title: Architectural historian

infobox.value: * [Architectural history](/wiki/Architectural_history "Architectural history") * [Art history](/wiki/Art_history "Art history") * [Archaeology](/wiki/Archaeology "Archaeology") * [History of architecture](/wiki/History_of_architecture "History of architecture")
Historical knowledge, Heritage conservation and Management skills

is a person who studies and writes about the [history of architecture](/wiki/History_of_architecture "History of architecture"), and is regarded as an [authority](/wiki/Authority "Authority") on it.[[1]](#cite_note-CB06-1)

This employment category is similar to Main Street management listed below and is interrelated with the following historical research and evaluation category. Professionals in the present field are primarily concerned with the planning and administration of preservation programs, providing technical support to the community and attending public meetings. Positions in this category are typically more office based and require more interpersonal skills than the following category.

This field is the counterpart to preservation planning and administration, above. It involves completing field surveys, conducting research and completing the reports reviewed by state and local government agencies. Compared to preservation planning and administration this field is less office oriented, requiring more on-site work and travel.
This field is analogous with museum curatorship but often includes aspects of historical research and evaluation, above and/or historic foundation management and administration, below.

Positions in this field are rarely entry level and generally require experience in nonprofit organization administration coupled with a background in architectural history. In many cases duties include those of historic site management as well.

Professionals in this field implement and manage downtown revitalization and preservation programs known as Main Street programs. These programs are assisted by the National Trust for Historic Preservation, National Main Street Center. In addition to sharing many of the activities in the preservation planning and administration category, Main Street managers are also involved with marketing and fundraising.

Crist, B (2006), [*Careers for Historians: Architectural Historians*](https://web.archive.org/web/20110604080751/http://www.ncmuseumofhistory.org/fko/careersforhistorians/cfh.architecturalhistorian.pdf) (PDF), North Carolina Museum of History, p. 1, archived from [the original](http://ncmuseumofhistory.org/fko/careersforhistorians/cfh.architecturalhistorian.pdf) (PDF) on 4 June 2011, retrieved 17 July 2011, "."

US Department of Interior (2011). ["ARCHEOLOGY AND HISTORIC PRESERVATION:Secretary of the Interior's Standards and Guidelines [As Amended and Annotated]"](https://web.archive.org/web/20070702000834/http://www.nps.gov/history/local-law/arch_stnds_9.htm). National Park Service. Archived from [the original](http://www.nps.gov/history/local-law/arch_stnds_9.htm) on July 2, 2007. Retrieved 2 August 2011.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Person who studies the history of architecture

Main article: [List of architectural historians](/wiki/List_of_architectural_historians "List of architectural historians")

Architectural historian

| Occupation | |
| --- | --- |
| Names | Architectural historian |
| Occupation type | [Profession](/wiki/Profession "Profession") |
| Activity sectors | * [Architectural history](/wiki/Architectural_history "Architectural history") * [Art history](/wiki/Art_history "Art history") * [Archaeology](/wiki/Archaeology "Archaeology") * [History of architecture](/wiki/History_of_architecture "History of architecture") |
| Description | |
| Competencies | Historical knowledge, Heritage conservation and Management skills |
| Education required | see [professional requirements](#Professional_requirements) |
| Related jobs | [Architect](/wiki/Architect "Architect") [Art Historian](/wiki/Art_Historian "Art Historian")   [Archaeologist](/wiki/Archaeologist "Archaeologist") |

An **architectural historian** is a person who studies and writes about the [history of architecture](/wiki/History_of_architecture "History of architecture"), and is regarded as an [authority](/wiki/Authority "Authority") on it.[[1]](#cite_note-CB06-1)

## Professional requirements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Architectural_historian&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Professional requirements")]

As many architectural historians are employed at universities and other facilities for post-secondary education, in addition to bachelor's degree, it is normal for colleges and universities to require the PhD degree for new full-time hires and a master's degree for part-timers.

### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Architectural_historian&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: United States")]

According to [Secretary of the Interior](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_the_Interior "United States Secretary of the Interior")'s Guidelines[[2]](#cite_note-CW01-2) the minimum professional qualifications in architectural history are a [graduate degree](/wiki/Graduate_degree "Graduate degree") in [architectural history](/wiki/Architectural_history "Architectural history"), [art history](/wiki/Art_history "Art history"), [historic preservation](/wiki/Historic_preservation "Historic preservation"), or closely related field, with coursework in American architectural history, or a [bachelor's degree](/wiki/Bachelor%27s_degree "Bachelor's degree") in [architectural history](/wiki/Architectural_history "Architectural history"), [art history](/wiki/Art_history "Art history"), [historic preservation](/wiki/Historic_preservation "Historic preservation") or closely related field plus one of the following:

* At least two years of full-time experience in research, writing, or teaching in American architectural history or restoration architecture with an academic institution, historical organization or agency, [museum](/wiki/Museum "Museum"), or other professional institution; or
* Substantial contribution through research and publication to the body of scholarly knowledge in the field of American architectural history.

## Profession

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Architectural_historian&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Profession")]

Professional architectural historians typically work in colleges and universities, archival centers, government agencies, museums, and as freelance writers and consultants. In broad terms, they can be grouped into following two categories:

### Academic titles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Architectural_historian&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Academic titles")]

Common titles and job descriptions within Universities and research organizations might be as follows:

* Research Coordinator
* Professor in Architectural History
* Senior Lecturer in Architectural History
* Lecturer in Architectural History
* Researcher in Architectural History
* Research Associate in Architectural History
* Research Assistant in Architectural History

### Non-academic titles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Architectural_historian&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Non-academic titles")]

Most non-academic positions in architectural history can be grouped into one of the following five categories[[3]](#cite_note-CW03-3)...

* **Preservation Planning and Administration**

:   This employment category is similar to Main Street management listed below and is interrelated with the following historical research and evaluation category. Professionals in the present field are primarily concerned with the planning and administration of preservation programs, providing technical support to the community and attending public meetings. Positions in this category are typically more office based and require more interpersonal skills than the following category.
:   Principal employers are state and local government agencies, including historic preservation offices and nonprofit organizations.

* **Historical Research and Evaluation**

:   This field is the counterpart to preservation planning and administration, above. It involves completing field surveys, conducting research and completing the reports reviewed by state and local government agencies. Compared to preservation planning and administration this field is less office oriented, requiring more on-site work and travel.
:   Principal employers are architectural firms, cultural resource firms and government agencies.

* **Historic Site Management and Curatorship**

:   This field is analogous with museum curatorship but often includes aspects of historical research and evaluation, above and/or historic foundation management and administration, below.
:   Principal employers are local governments or private, nonprofit organizations.

* **Historic Foundation Management and Administration**

:   Positions in this field are rarely entry level and generally require experience in nonprofit organization administration coupled with a background in architectural history. In many cases duties include those of historic site management as well.
:   Principal employers are historic foundations.

* **Main Street Management**

:   Professionals in this field implement and manage downtown revitalization and preservation programs known as Main Street programs. These programs are assisted by the National Trust for Historic Preservation, National Main Street Center. In addition to sharing many of the activities in the preservation planning and administration category, Main Street managers are also involved with marketing and fundraising.
:   Principal employers are nonprofit organizations.

## Professional organizations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Architectural_historian&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Professional organizations")]

* [Society of Architectural Historians](/wiki/Society_of_Architectural_Historians "Society of Architectural Historians")
* [Society for the Study of Architecture in Canada](/w/index.php?title=Society_for_the_Study_of_Architecture_in_Canada&action=edit&redlink=1 "Society for the Study of Architecture in Canada (page does not exist)")
* [The Society of Architectural Historians of Great Britain](/wiki/Society_of_Architectural_Historians_of_Great_Britain "Society of Architectural Historians of Great Britain")
* [The Society of Architectural Historians, Australia and New Zealand](/wiki/SAHANZ "SAHANZ")

## Salaries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Architectural_historian&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Salaries")]

Following are averages of salary ranges as listed in position announcements, excluding additional benefits. The upper salary level listed in such announcements may represent qualifications exceeding the minimum requirements specified for the position.

### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Architectural_historian&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: United States")]

According to a survey conducted by the architectural history department, [Savannah College of Art and Design](/wiki/Savannah_College_of_Art_and_Design "Savannah College of Art and Design"), on professional career opportunities in [architectural history](/wiki/Architectural_history "Architectural history"), was compiled in January 2010 from positions listed January–December 2009,[[3]](#cite_note-CW03-3) averages of salary ranges in [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") are below.

Positions requiring:

* a [B.A.](/wiki/Bachelor_of_Arts "Bachelor of Arts") in [architectural history](/wiki/Architectural_history "Architectural history") or a related field and minimal experience: $30,000-$38,500
* an [M.A.](/wiki/Master_of_Arts "Master of Arts") or a [B.A.](/wiki/Bachelor_of_Arts "Bachelor of Arts") plus two years of relevant experience, [M.A.](/wiki/Master_of_Arts "Master of Arts") frequently preferred. Most positions specify an additional two to three years of experience: $40,500-$55,000.
* or preferring an [M.A.](/wiki/Master_of_Arts "Master of Arts") with a minimum of three to five years of experience: $48,500-$71,000.
* an advanced degree and more than five years of experience: $61,500- $79,000.
